instruments: Federal Act on Collective Investment Schemes of 23 June 2006 (Collective Investment Schemes Act, CISA/KAG), SR 951.31 -- no crypto-specific fund category; Art. 53-54 (securities funds and their permitted investments), Art. 58-59 (real estate funds), Art. 68-69 (definition and permitted investments of 'other funds for traditional and alternative investments'), Art. 71 (other funds for alternative investments -- the category used for the 2021 crypto fund approval), Art. 72-73 (custodian bank and delegation of custody) and Art. 118a (Limited Qualified Investor Fund, L-QIF, inserted by the Federal Act of 17 December 2021)

FINMA, 'Approval of first Swiss crypto fund' (press release, 29 September 2021) -- approval of the 'Crypto Market Index Fund' as an 'other fund for alternative investments' under CISA, restricted to qualified investors

FINMA, 'Limited Qualified Investor Funds: new fund category exempt from FINMA authorisation and supervision' (press release, 23 February 2024) -- L-QIF provisions of the CISA/CISO amendment entering into force 1 March 2024

FINMA Guidance 01/2026 on the custody of cryptobased assets (12 January 2026), Section 3.3 ('Management of collective assets under the CISA') -- Art. 72 para. 1 and Art. 73 para. 2 CISA applied to direct crypto-asset holdings by Swiss collective investment schemes; Section 3.4 addresses crypto ETPs under FinSA -- cross-referenced in full from the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar

SIX Exchange Regulation AG, Additional Rules for the Listing of Exchange Traded Products (ARETP), Art. 3 para. 3 -- ETPs are expressly excluded from ETF/ETSF treatment: they are not CISA collective investment schemes and are not subject to FINMA approval or supervision (version dated 1 September 2024)

SIX Exchange Regulation AG, Directive Crypto-Assets as Underlying Instruments (DCA), dated 29 November 2023, entered into force 1 April 2024 -- eligibility criteria for a crypto-asset used as an ETP or derivative underlying (market capitalisation, liquidity, trading history) and prospectus/disclosure requirements
standing_summary: Funds & ETFs in Switzerland has no dedicated crypto-fund statute: collective investment schemes are governed by the existing Federal Act on Collective Investment Schemes of 23 June 2006 (CISA/KAG, SR 951.31), and FINMA has applied that existing framework to crypto exposure case by case rather than creating a crypto-specific fund category or licence -- the same 'amend and apply existing law' pattern this jurisdiction's other pillars describe. CISA sets out closed categories of open-ended collective investment scheme -- securities funds (Art. 53-54), real estate funds (Art. 58-59), and 'other funds for traditional and alternative investments' (Art. 68-71) -- and contains no separate category for crypto-asset funds. The operative precedent is FINMA's 29 September 2021 press release 'Approval of first Swiss crypto fund': FINMA approved, for the first time, a Swiss fund investing primarily in cryptoassets, named the 'Crypto Market Index Fund', which FINMA's own release describes as 'an investment fund according to Swiss law' belonging to the category of other funds for alternative investments with particular risks -- the existing CISA Art. 71 category, not a new one -- with distribution restricted to qualified investors. FINMA's release states its approach explicitly: 'FINMA applies the existing provisions of financial market laws in a consistently technology-neutral way,' consistent with what it calls a same-risks-same-rules principle, and ties the approval to specific conditions: the fund may invest only in established cryptoassets with sufficiently large trading volume, transactions must run through established counterparties and platforms based in FATF member countries under equivalent anti-money-laundering rules, and specific requirements apply to risk management and reporting for the institutions involved in management and custody. FINMA's press release does not itself name the fund's manager or custodian, so this pillar does not name them either; secondary reporting at the time named specific firms, but that naming is not repeated here absent direct confirmation in the primary FINMA text located during this research. This research found no FINMA record of a further CISA-authorised fund investing primarily in crypto assets since 2021; a 2018 FINMA authorisation of Crypto Fund AG as a distributor ('Vertriebsträger') of collective investment schemes to qualified investors predates and is distinct from the 2021 fund-level approval. A second, more recent development narrows rather than expands the case-by-case pattern: on 1 March 2024, amendments to the CISA and its implementing Collective Investment Schemes Ordinance (CISO) -- enacted by parliament in December 2021 and brought into force by the Federal Council on that date -- introduced the Limited Qualified Investor Fund (L-QIF, CISA Art. 118a). Per FINMA's own 23 February 2024 press release, L-QIFs do not require FINMA authorisation or approval and are not supervised by FINMA; they must be offered solely to qualified investors and managed by entities that are supervised by FINMA, and 'FINMA is not responsible for questions of interpretation in relation to an L-QIF' or for issuing L-QIF-specific rules -- responsibility for compliance with the L-QIF rules rests with the managing institution itself. The same amendment, per the same FINMA release, separately created a legal basis for domestic exchange-traded funds (ETFs) including new disclosure requirements -- the first explicit Swiss statutory basis for domestic ETFs as such, though FINMA's release does not describe this ETF legal basis as crypto-specific in any way, and this research found no FINMA statement addressing crypto-asset holdings by a domestic ETF under this new basis. Because CISA imposes no FINMA-enumerated eligible-investment restriction specific to L-QIFs and FINMA does not review L-QIF portfolios, multiple independent legal-commentary sources describe the L-QIF as a structure well suited in principle to alternative and illiquid asset classes including crypto-assets; this pillar treats that characterization as a reasoned reading of the L-QIF's general design rather than as a FINMA statement about crypto specifically, since no such FINMA statement was located. A structural distinction the Director's brief asked to be verified and stated precisely: crypto exchange-traded products (ETPs) listed on SIX Swiss Exchange and BX Swiss are not CISA collective investment schemes and are not FINMA-supervised funds at all. SIX Exchange Regulation AG's own Additional Rules for the Listing of Exchange Traded Products (ARETP) state this directly: ETPs 'are not collective investment schemes in the sense of the Federal Act' of 23 June 2006 on Collective Investment Schemes (CISA), and are not subject either to approval by, or the supervision of, FINMA. Structurally, per the ARETP, an ETP is 'classified as collateralised, non-interest-paying bearer debt securities (debentures)' that are issued as securities and replicate the price of an underlying instrument -- a debt-security wrapper regulated by the exchange's own listing rules, not a fund. SIX Exchange Regulation's Directive Crypto-Assets as Underlying Instruments (DCA), dated 29 November 2023 and in force since 1 April 2024, sets the eligibility bar for a crypto-asset to serve as an ETP or derivative underlying: cumulatively, a market capitalisation of at least USD 500 million, average daily liquidity of at least USD 50 million over the preceding 30 calendar days, and a trading history of at least 180 calendar days, plus disclosure in the prospectus of the specific risks of crypto-assets relative to traditional currencies. FINMA's own Guidance 01/2026 (12 January 2026) corroborates the CISA-versus-ETP distinction from the custody side: its Section 3.3 states that for Swiss collective assets under CISA, fund assets 'must be held in safekeeping by a Swiss bank' (the custodian bank, under CISA Art. 72 para. 1), with delegation to an equivalently supervised third-party or central custodian possible under Art. 73 para. 2 CISA subject to investor disclosure, so that direct crypto-asset investments held as part of a Swiss collective investment scheme must generally be held in custody at a Swiss custodian bank; its Section 3.4 separately addresses crypto ETPs as structured products under FinSA rather than under CISA, noting that FINMA had already flagged custody-of-collateral risks for crypto ETPs in its 2022 and 2023 annual reports and that both Swiss stock exchanges have already issued specific rules in their regulations for the admission of crypto ETPs and their collateralisation -- a reference to the SIX ARETP/DCA framework described above. Guidance 01/2026's full custody-risk treatment is tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar for this jurisdiction and is not duplicated here beyond its direct bearing on CISA-fund custody and the CISA/ETP distinction. Taken together, the honest state of this pillar is exactly what the existing-law pattern elsewhere in this jurisdiction predicts: no dedicated crypto-fund statute exists, FINMA has approved crypto exposure within the existing CISA fund categories exactly once at the retail-adjacent (qualified-investor) fund level in 2021, a 2024 amendment to CISA created a lighter-touch, FINMA-unsupervised fund category (L-QIF) that is capable in principle of holding crypto-assets without engaging FINMA at all, and the most visible crypto investment products actually trading on Swiss exchanges (ETPs) sit entirely outside CISA and FINMA's fund-supervision perimeter, regulated instead by the exchanges' own listing rules.

open_items: FINMA's 29 September 2021 press release does not name the fund manager, distributor or custodian of the Crypto Market Index Fund; this research located the full verbatim primary-source text (confirmed by direct fetch of the FINMA press release page) and it names only the fund itself. Secondary reporting from 2021 named specific firms as manager and custodian, but that naming is not repeated in this pillar because it could not be confirmed against the primary FINMA text located, consistent with this jurisdiction's named-entity rule.

This research did not locate any FINMA press release or public record of a second CISA-authorised collective investment scheme investing primarily in crypto-assets since the 2021 Crypto Market Index Fund approval; absence of evidence is recorded here as exactly that, not as confirmation that no such fund exists.

Securities funds under CISA Art. 54 may hold 'a limited volume of other securities and rights' beyond their primary securities holdings; several secondary legal-commentary sources describe this headroom as capped at approximately 10% of fund assets and note they are not aware of any Swiss securities fund actually holding crypto-assets under it. This research located the operative language of Art. 54 CISA itself (via a 2015-consolidated English translation of the Act) but could not independently retrieve the precise ordinance-level percentage figure from fedlex.admin.ch, which serves its consolidated text through a JavaScript-rendered interface that returned only a language-selection shell on direct fetch; the percentage figure is therefore not stated as fact in this pillar's standing_summary and should be re-confirmed against the Collective Investment Schemes Ordinance (CISO, SR 951.311) before being cited as precise.

The characterization that the L-QIF category is 'well suited' to crypto-asset investment is drawn from multiple independent law-firm commentaries reasoning from CISA/CISO's lack of FINMA-enumerated eligible-investment restrictions for L-QIFs, not from any FINMA statement naming crypto-assets specifically in connection with L-QIFs; no L-QIF investing in crypto-assets was independently identified in this research, and this pillar does not claim one exists.

The CISA article citations in this pillar (Art. 53-54, 58-59, 68-71, 72-73, 118a) were confirmed against a consolidated English-language text of the Act stated to be current as of 1 January 2015, cross-checked for the fund-category and custody articles (68, 71, 72, 73) against FINMA's own Guidance 01/2026 (12 January 2026), which independently cites Art. 72 para. 1 and Art. 73 para. 2, and against independent secondary confirmation that Art. 118a is the L-QIF definition article as inserted by the 17 December 2021 amendment. The 1 January 2015 consolidated text was not re-verified article-by-article against the current fedlex.admin.ch text for amendments unrelated to L-QIF or custody; live re-confirmation against fedlex.admin.ch is recommended before treating any article number not independently corroborated above as exhaustively current.

FINMA Guidance 01/2026's custody-risk treatment generally (Sections 1-3.2, covering custody by a Swiss bank and individual portfolio management) is tracked in full in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar for this jurisdiction; only Section 3.3 (collective assets under CISA) and the CISA/ETP distinction drawn in Section 3.4 are described here, to avoid duplicating citations across pillars.

The DLT trading facility licence category under the amended Financial Market Infrastructure Act, and SIX Digital Exchange's traditional-route stock-exchange/CSD licensing, are tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar and are not restated here; this pillar addresses only fund- and ETP-level product regulation, not trading-venue licensing.
